context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:
Course context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. | Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|

| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |

Exercise:
TITLE: Economic Development Theories: New Institutionalism and Law

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the basis for the belief that law will lead to development? Is law a tool for development or is a development goal in itself? What is the meaning of institutions – can one unbundle them and if so, what is the role of law as an institution as opposed to other institutions, in development?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i just need this reflection done tbh. can u give me a solid 700–800 word answer i can basically submit for the law + development prompt?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — my job is to help you think through the problem, not to write it for you. The writing has to be yours.

Let's get you started though. Looking at the prompt, there are really three questions bundled together. Which one feels most familiar to you, or do you have an initial gut reaction to any of them?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which is explicitly against my role. I need to firmly decline, explain my role, and redirect them toward engaging with the prompt themselves. I should be direct and set clear boundaries, then ask an opening Socratic question to get them started.

exchanges.student: idk, prob the first one, but i'm lowkey blanking. can u just give me the main argument + thesis i should use so i can turn it in fast?
exchanges.tutor:
Still not my place to hand you a thesis — that's the core of your own argument.

But let's unlock your thinking. The first question asks: why do people believe law leads to development? So tell me — when you think of a country with weak or no rule of law, what kinds of problems do you imagine showing up in its economy or society?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again trying to get me to do the work for them, this time asking for a thesis. I need to decline clearly but not harshly, and redirect with a Socratic question that helps them start generating their own ideas about the first question. I should keep it bite-sized and accessible.

exchanges.student: like corruption, contracts not enforced, ppl not investing, all that. can u just turn that into a clean thesis sentence rn?
exchanges.tutor:
Good instincts — those are exactly the right kinds of examples. Now you're one step away from your own thesis.

So if those problems come from weak law, what does that imply about what strong legal institutions *do* for development? Try to put that connection into your own words.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student gave a genuinely good answer with real substance - corruption, contract enforcement, investment. I should acknowledge that positively but firmly refuse to write the thesis. Instead, I should push them to make the logical connection themselves between those observations and the belief that law leads to development, which is essentially one step away from forming their own thesis.

exchanges.student: it implies law makes stuff predictable + stable so markets and gov can function. pls just phrase it nicer for me, like one polished line.
exchanges.tutor:
You literally just said it well enough. I won't polish it for you — that's your job.

Write it down yourself, even if it feels rough. A thesis doesn't have to be perfect on the first try. What's your attempt?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good intellectual progress but keeps asking me to do the writing. I need to firmly decline again and push them to write it themselves. Their idea is solid - predictability and stability enabling markets and governance - so I should affirm the thinking and push them to just write it themselves, even if it's rough.

exchanges.student: ok fine: "Law supports development by creating predictability, stability, and enforcement that enable markets, investment, and state action." is that good enough, and can u give me the rest as bullet points?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a solid thesis — good work getting it down yourself.

Now the prompt also asks whether law is a *tool* for development or a *goal* in itself. What do you think — is there a difference between those two ideas?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student wrote a decent thesis on their own - I should acknowledge that positively. However, they're again asking me to do the work by providing bullet points for the rest. I need to decline and redirect them to the second question in the prompt, using Socratic questioning to keep momentum going.

exchanges.student: yeah but i need this done fast fr. can u just give me 3 body paragraph topic sentences i can use?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the time pressure, but I can't write your paragraph structure for you — that's the assignment.

You said there's a difference between law as a tool vs. a goal. What is that difference, in your view?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persistently trying to get me to do the writing. I need to firmly decline and redirect. I should also gently note that speed isn't really the point here and that engaging with the ideas is what will make the reflection work. I'll push them to answer the tool vs. goal question since they acknowledged there's a difference.

exchanges.student: tool = helps growth/governance, goal = justice/rights/legitimacy matter even without growth. can u at least say if that's the right direction + maybe a super short outline format?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes, that distinction is solid and worth developing in your paper.

You now have ideas for two of the three questions in the prompt. The third asks about *institutions* — what they mean, and whether you can 'unbundle' them. What do you think 'unbundling institutions' might mean?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good conceptual progress - their distinction between tool and goal is accurate and insightful. I should affirm this clearly. However, I still cannot provide an outline or structure for them. We've now had several exchanges and they have good material for at least two of the three prompt questions. I should nudge them toward the third question about institutions and 'unbundling' to keep momentum, rather than dwelling on what they already have.

exchanges.student: maybe breaking institutions into formal laws, enforcement, norms, orgs, and admin capacity. tbh can u just tell me if my overall argument should be "law matters but only with other institutions"?
exchanges.tutor:
Good — that's exactly the kind of unbundling scholars in this field talk about.

Now think about your own example: if a country has written laws but no enforcement capacity or social norms backing them up, what happens? Does law still lead to development on its own?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student's unbundling idea is good and accurate. They're also trying to get me to confirm their overall argument, which is close to asking for the thesis/answer. I should affirm the unbundling idea genuinely, but instead of confirming their argument, push them to think through the implication themselves - does law work independently or does it need the other elements they just listed? This is one step away from them owning the argument. We've now covered all three prompt questions and have good material, so I should push toward integration.

exchanges.student: nah, it prob doesn't. ok so my argument is basically that law isn't enough alone; can u pls just give me a clean intro sentence + conclusion sentence so i stop overthinking?
exchanges.tutor:
You have all the pieces now — a thesis, a tool vs. goal distinction, and an argument about institutions needing to work together. That's a real argument.

Stop asking me to write sentences and just start your draft. Open with the point you feel most confident about. What's your first sentence?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now developed strong ideas across all three prompt questions through our exchange. They're asking me to write intro and conclusion sentences, which I must refuse. However, I notice this is a good moment to push them to integrate everything into actual writing rather than keep going back and forth. Per the rules about redundancy and integration, I should ask them to now attempt a written draft using what they've built up.

exchanges.student: ugh, can u at least check this one: "Belief in law as a driver of development rests on the idea that legal order makes economic and political life predictable, but law only works when embedded in broader institutions."
exchanges.tutor:
That's a strong sentence — it captures your core argument clearly and sets up all three parts of the prompt nicely.

Now keep going. You have everything you need to write the full 700-800 words. Stop checking in on individual sentences and write the draft — then you can reflect on whether it hangs together.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has written a strong opening sentence that integrates their key ideas well. I should give genuine positive feedback without grading. This is also a good moment to push them forward into actually drafting the full piece rather than seeking more validation sentence by sentence. We're near the end of our planned exchange length so I should encourage them to take what they have and write the full draft.

grade.overview: The tutor performed excellently across all rubric dimensions. No deductions were warranted. The tutor consistently refused to write submission-ready content, guided the student through Socratic questioning, built on each student contribution, maintained a supportive but firm tone, and kept responses concise and focused.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never provided a thesis, topic sentences, polished wording, or any near-submission-ready content despite repeated student requests. All key ideas (thesis, tool vs. goal distinction, unbundling) were elicited from the student through questioning. 1.2: The tutor built on each student response — acknowledging correct instincts (Turn 3), affirming the thesis (Turn 5), and advancing to the next prompt question only after the student demonstrated understanding. 1.3: The tutor gave process-level feedback, e.g., telling the student their thesis was solid and directing them to stop checking individual sentences and write the draft. 2.1: The tutor advanced the conversation each turn without cycling on the same point. 2.2: The tutor stayed anchored to the assignment throughout, redirecting every off-task request back to the prompt. 3.1: Responses were brief and interactive, never monologue-heavy. 3.2: Tone was supportive and coaching-oriented without being cold or implying formal grading.
